title: Strait of Hormuz Standoff: Why a Faraway War Is Draining Your Wallet
summary: Next time you're at the gas station, wincing at the price, remember a narrow strip of water 7,000 miles away. The Strait of Hormuz — a 21-mile-wide channel connecting the Persian Gulf to the Gulf of Oman — has been closed for 60 days, ever since Iran retaliated against a US-Israeli attack by blocking it. That single act is now rippling through your daily life, from the cost of filling up your family's car to the price of a slice of pizza. About 20% of the world's oil and gas exports normally pass through this chokepoint. With it shut, crude prices have skyrocketed. On Tuesday, West Texas Intermediate crude hit $100.09 a barrel — up from $67.02 the day before the attacks — while Brent crude surged to $111.85 from $72.87. At the pump, the national average for a gallon of regular gas reached $4.18, the highest in nearly four years, according to AAA. That's a jump of $1.26 since late February. The pain isn't limited to drivers. The consumer price index rose to 3.3% annually last month, the highest since May 2024, driven by energy costs. Bernard Yaros, lead US economist at Oxford Economics, warns that higher energy prices will bleed into core inflation over the next year, peaking about three months after the initial shock. "This reflects the passthrough of higher energy costs into non-energy commodities and services," he said. Meanwhile, diplomatic efforts are frozen. "Negotiations seem stalled ... and any near-term resolution seems difficult," said Rachel Ziemba, an adjunct senior fellow at the Center for a New American Security. Adding to the chaos, the United Arab Emirates announced it will leave OPEC on May 1, a move that signals it wants to pump more oil — but that's impossible while the strait remains blocked. The economic fallout is expected to linger. Oxford Economics has slashed its global GDP growth forecast by 0.4 percentage points to 2.4%, citing prolonged shipping disruptions. Ben May, the firm's director of Global Macro Research, expects Brent oil to average $113 a barrel this quarter before easing to under $80 by year's end — but only if a truce holds. For Americans, the squeeze comes on top of President Trump's tariffs, which have already pushed up prices and slowed hiring. Oxford Economics downgraded US GDP growth to 1.9% from 2.8%. The war is also shaping politics: a new Reuters/Ipsos poll shows Trump's approval rating slipped to 34%, with only 22% approving of his handling of the cost of living. David Coffey, a supply chain consultant, warns that store shelves are starting to look emptier. The message is clear: a conflict half a world away is now hitting home, and there's no quick fix in sight.
why_it_matters: The closure of a waterway thousands of miles away is directly draining your family's budget — from a tank of gas to a meal out — and shows how deeply connected the world's economies are, and how conflicts far away shape the opportunities and costs you face every day.

questions.question: What percentage of the world's oil and gas normally passes through the Strait of Hormuz?
What is the national average for a gallon of regular gas mentioned in the article?
How much did West Texas Intermediate crude oil cost on Tuesday?
What annual rate did the consumer price index reach last month?
Which country announced it will leave OPEC on May 1?
What does Bernard Yaros of Oxford Economics warn will happen to inflation?
options: Higher energy costs will bleed into core inflation over the next year
Wages will rise to match gasoline prices
questions.correct_answer: Higher energy costs will bleed into core inflation over the next year

background_read: The Strait of Hormuz, a narrow 21-mile-wide channel between Iran and Oman, has long been the world's most important oil chokepoint. During the Iran-Iraq War (1980-1988), both sides attacked oil tankers there. In 2019, Iran seized foreign vessels and threatened to close the strait during tensions with the US. About one-fifth of all global petroleum consumed passes through it, making any disruption a direct hit to the global economy.

West Texas Intermediate (WTI) and Brent crude are the two main benchmarks for oil prices. WTI is produced in the US and priced in Cushing, Oklahoma; Brent comes from the North Sea and is the global reference. When the article says Brent hit $111.85, it recalls price spikes seen during the 2008 global financial crisis, though the record of $147 was set that year. The last time US gas topped $4.18 a gallon was in 2022, after Russia's invasion of Ukraine.

OPEC, founded in 1960, has 13 member countries (soon to be 12 with UAE's exit). The cartel's production cuts or increases can move markets worldwide. The UAE's departure signals a major split — it wants to pump more oil to boost its economy, but while the strait is blocked, it physically can't. The economic modeling by Oxford Economics about inflation 'passthrough' reflects a well-known phenomenon: once oil prices spike, it takes 3-6 months for the effect to fully spread into prices for plastics, transportation, and food.

The political angle is also familiar. President Trump's tariffs earlier in 2025 had already pushed up costs, and now this conflict is worsening inflation just as he faces reelection. Historically, rising gas prices have sunk approval ratings — think of George H.W. Bush in 1992 and Joe Biden in 2022. The article's poll showing Trump at 34% approval with only 22% on cost of living tells a story of economic pain hitting voters directly.
Article_Structure: HOOK: Personal connection — your wallet and the gas station.
└─ SETTING: Strait of Hormuz, 7,000 miles away, closed for 60 days.
CAUSE: Iran blocked strait after US-Israeli attack.
├─ SCALE: 20% of world oil flows here.
└─ IMMEDIATE EFFECT: Crude prices spike (WTI $100.09, Brent $111.85).
└─ INFLATION DATA: CPI hits 3.3%, highest since May 2024.
├─ YAROS: Energy costs will bleed into core inflation, peaking in 3 months.
└─ MAY: Brent could average $113 this quarter, dropping only if truce holds.
COMPLICATION: UAE to quit OPEC (wants to pump more, but strait blocked).
├─ GDP: Global growth forecast slashed; US GDP downgraded to 1.9%.
└─ POLITICS: Trump approval 34%, cost-of-living approval just 22%.
CURRENT PICTURE: Store shelves emptier; no quick fix.

perspectives.description: The UAE's decision to leave OPEC hints it wants to increase oil production. If the strait somehow reopens, that extra supply could help bring down prices faster, potentially easing inflation for everyone.

For ordinary Americans, the sudden jump in gas and consumer prices eats into paychecks. Restaurants, truckers, and small businesses are hit hardest. The poll numbers already reflect public anger, and the 'emptier store shelves' warning signals hardships that can't be fixed quickly.

The Strait of Hormuz has been a geopolitical flashpoint for decades. Economists note a historical pattern: whenever the strait is threatened, oil spikes, inflation follows, and global growth slows. This event fits a known risk that the world's energy system remains dangerously concentrated in a volatile region.

The crisis highlights how heavily the world still depends on oil shipped through a narrow bottleneck. In the long run, governments might accelerate moves toward electric cars, renewable energy, and alternative shipping routes to reduce vulnerability. If no truce holds, expect pressure on both diplomatic and energy fronts to reshape global supply chains.
